mission.title: Install and Configure vLLM
mission.description: vLLM is a high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for large language models (LLMs). It is ideal for applications requiring fast and scalable LLM serving, leveraging advanced techniques for memory management and request batching. Key trade-offs include the need for specific hardware configurations to fully utilize its capabilities.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the vLLM Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install vLLM using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add vllm https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/releases/download/v0.17.0

```

> This command adds the vLLM repository with the specified release.

steps.title: Install Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for vLLM to define its custom resources in Kubernetes. This step ensures that the required CRDs are installed.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/v0.17.0/deploy/crds/vllm.io_vllms_crd.yaml

```

> This command installs the vLLM CRDs.

steps.title: Install vLLM using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs the vLLM application in the specified namespace using Helm, allowing you to manage it easily.

```bash

helm install vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --create-namespace --version 0.17.0

```

> This command installs vLLM in the 'vllm' namespace.

steps.title: Create an Example Custom Resource
steps.description:
Creating a custom resource for vLLM is essential to configure and deploy your LLM model. This step sets up an example resource.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1alpha1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

```

> This command creates an example vLLM resource for a GPT-3 model.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1alpha1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 2
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Custom Resource
steps.description:
After creating the custom resource, it's important to verify that it is running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get vllms -n vllm

```

> This command lists all vLLM instances in the 'vllm' namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall vLLM
uninstall.description:
This step removes the vLLM installation from your cluster. Be aware that this will delete all associated resources and may lead to data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall vllm --namespace vllm

kubectl delete namespace vllm

```

> This command uninstalls the vLLM release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade vLLM
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any important data. This process includes draining the pods, upgrading, and verifying the installation.

```bash

kubectl drain <pod-name> --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --version 0.17.0

kubectl get vllms -n vllm

```

> Replace <pod-name> with the name of your vLLM pod to safely drain it.
> This command upgrades vLLM to the specified version.
> Verify that the vLLM instance is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'vllm.io/v1alpha1' not found
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The CRDs may not be installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/v0.17.0/deploy/crds/vllm.io_vllms_crd.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image' when deploying vLLM
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified container image may not exist or is inaccessible.

**Fix:**
Check the image name and ensure it is available in the specified registry.

troubleshooting.title: Pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in the custom resource or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the pod using kubectl logs <pod-name> and adjust the resource requests accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU' when creating pods
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources allocated for the vLLM pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up your cluster or adjust the resource requests in your custom resource definition.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new models and improved memory management features.
versionNotes.deprecations: No specific deprecations noted in this release.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see output similar to 'NAME              READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE' with your vLLM instance listed as '1/1' under the READY column.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified tools installed.
